coredata.dc:title: Effective removal of organic pollution by using sonochemical prepared LaFeO3 perovskite under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Mehdizadeh, Pourya
Amiri, Omid
Rashki, Somaye
Salavati-Niasari, Masoud
Salimian, Morteza
Foong, Loke Kok
coredata.dc:description: In the present work, LaFeO3 perovskite was prepared via ultrasonic probe with power of 60 W and frequency of 18 KHz. LaFeO3 nanorods were formed when sonication time was 20 min. In this research, green materials including corn, starch, and rice were used to control the size, morphology, and purity of final products. As-prepared LaFeO3 nanostructures were used to purify water containing organic contaminants. LaFeO3 nanostructures prepared by using corn, starch, and rice showed higher photocatalytic activity compare to LaFeO3 nanostructures without natural capping agents. Using corn increased degradation efficiency by 65% under visible light. XRD results show that Fe2O3 appeared as an impurity when starch was used to prepare LaFeO3 nanostructures. This impurity significantly boosts the degradation efficiency under UV light. Fe2O3 under UV light act as co-absorbent and boost efficiency by 43%. LaFeO3 nanostructures were characterized by XRD, EDX, SEM, CV, BET, TEM, DRS and FT-IR.
coredata.dcterms:subject: LaFeO3
Ultrasonic
Green chemistry
Sonochemistry
Water treatment
Degradation pathways

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD of sample 1–4. a) shows the XRD pattern for the sample without calcination, XRD pattern of sample calcinated at b) 500, c) 600, and d) 700 C. Pure LaFeO3 nanostructures were formed at 600 and 700 C.

a) Pure LaFeO3 was achieved when rice was used as capping agent, b) mixture of LaFeO3 and Fe2O3 was formed when starch was used as a capping agent, c) when corn was used as capping agent, pure LaFeO3 nanostructures were obtained.

The effect of sonication time on the morphology of LaFeO3 nanostructures. Rods like LaFeO3 nanostructures with an average length of 100 nm were formed when sonication time was 20 min (a). It seems LaFeO3 nano-rods divided into smaller pieces by increasing sonication power to 30 min (b), particles with 20–30 nm in diameters were formed when sonication time was 40 min (c).
SEM images for samples prepared at a, b) 40 W and c, d) 80 W. Changing sonication power to 80 W cause broken nanorods and produce particles with 20–80 nm in diameters.

The effect of the precipitating agent (NH3 and en) on the morphology and size of products. When NH3 was used as precipitating agent, rod like LaFeO3 nanostructures were formed with wide size distributions (a, b), Changing precipitating agent to en leads to form more aggregated rod like LaFeO3 nanostructures (c, d).

The effect of capping agents on the morphology and size of products. Using rice as a capping agent leads to produce nanoparticles with 20–60 nm in diameters (a). When starch was used as capping agent, it seems particles stuck together to form a small chain (b). The sponge like LaFeO3 nanostructures were obtained when corn was used as a capping agent (c).
TEM images LaFeO3 nanostructures prepared by using corn.
a–d EDX results for samples without capping agent, with corn, starch, and rice as capping agents, respectively.
a) Displays the FTIR spectra obtained from LaFeO3 powders by using corn as a capping agent, b) CV was measured for LaFeO3 nanostructures by using corn as capping agent at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1.
a) Adsorption-desorption curve, b) BET, and c) BJT of LaFeO3 perovskite by using corn as a capping agent.
DRS of sample calcinated at 700 C without capping agent (a) and sample prepared by using corn as a green capping agent (b).

The photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3 nanostructures under visible light. Sample without capping agent (red curve), a sample with corn (blue curve), starch (purple curve), and rice (green curve) as capping agents.

a, b) Degradation efficiency for erythrosine as organic pollution under UV irradiation in 90 min. c, d) photocatalytic activity of samples without and with different capping agents when organic pollution was eriochrome black T.
Structure of erythrosine and its solution a) after Vis irradiation and b) after UV irradiation, c) structure of eriochrome black T and its solution after UV irradiation.
A mechanism for photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3 nanostructures. It also illustrates how impurity increases photocatalytic activity under UV light.
Experimental detail for synthesis of LaFeO3 nanostructures.

head.ce:title: Effective removal of organic pollution by using sonochemical prepared LaFeO3 perovskite under visible light

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In the present work, LaFeO3 perovskite was prepared via ultrasonic probe with power of 60 W and frequency of 18 KHz. LaFeO3 nanorods were formed when sonication time was 20 min. In this research, green materials including corn, starch, and rice were used to control the size, morphology, and purity of final products. As-prepared LaFeO3 nanostructures were used to purify water containing organic contaminants. LaFeO3 nanostructures prepared by using corn, starch, and rice showed higher photocatalytic activity compare to LaFeO3 nanostructures without natural capping agents. Using corn increased degradation efficiency by 65% under visible light. XRD results show that Fe2O3 appeared as an impurity when starch was used to prepare LaFeO3 nanostructures. This impurity significantly boosts the degradation efficiency under UV light. Fe2O3 under UV light act as co-absorbent and boost efficiency by 43%. LaFeO3 nanostructures were characterized by XRD, EDX, SEM, CV, BET, TEM, DRS and FT-IR.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution becomes a serious global concern threatening the entire biosphere and affecting the lives of many millions of people around the world. Water pollution causes a variety of diseases and millions of people die annually because of illness related to the dirty water. It also leads to a decrease the available drinkable water worldwide. Delivering valuable solutions, which are easy to implement and affordable, often remains a challenge [1].

Today developing different industries such as painting, mining, and texture release more toxic pollutants in the environment, in various forms and concentrations. On the other hand, population growth leads to an increase in the demand for accessible water [2,3].

Water contaminants majorly consist of organic, inorganic, and macroscopic impurities. Implementation of wastewater reclamation is a promising solution in response to water issues. However, the main concern about this method is that persistent organic pollutants could still be present in treated water. Up to now, different water treatment methods such as adsorption [4–6], coagulation [7,8] and membrane separation [9,10] have been used to purify water from persistent organic pollutants. However, these methods only concentrate or change the dissolved organic contaminants from the water to a solid phase. Therefore these methods are not desirable due to additional cost and treatment steps to treat the secondary contaminants and regenerate the adsorbents [11,12]. Therefore, advanced oxidation processes have been used for the removal of organic contaminants. Advanced oxidation processes (AOP) referee to the process those very strong chemical oxidants were generated by the assistance of hydrogen peroxide, ozone, UV light or a catalyst. The generated hydroxyl and superoxide radicals are strong oxidants agents that could oxidize and decompose a variety of organic compounds. AOPs enjoy several advantages including the ability to mineralize organic compounds to green products, rapid degradation rate, and ability to operate under ambient temperature and pressure. Heterogeneous photocatalysis is the main class of AOP that uses semiconductor oxides as a photocatalyst [13–16].

Among semiconductors have been used till now, TiO2 has been the most studied compound due to its low production cost and good chemical stability. However, its large bandgap (3.2 eV) and low quantum efficiency limited its application [17,18]. Therefore researchers always are looking for alternatives [19–21]. Recently, Perovskite-type material with a general formula ABX3 has received great attention and developed for numerous applications such as oxygen separation [22] solar cell [23], solid oxide fuel cell [24], and their photoelectrochemical properties are gradually explored in water splitting and purification [25–28].

In the present work, we introduce LaFeO3 perovskite as a promising alternative for TiO2. We prepare LaFeO3 nanostructures with a simple and new sonochemistry method. Natural and green capping agents including rice, starch, and corn were used as a capping agent. Beside, optical and electrochemical properties of as-prepared LaFeO3 nanostructures were studied.

To the synthesis of LaFeO3 nanostructures, 0.2 g of LaCl3·7H2O (purchased from Sigma Aldrich) and 0.21 g of Fe(NO3)3·9H2O (purchased from Sigma Aldrich) were dissolved in 20 mL distilled water separately. Then they were mixed in 100 mL beaker. Natural capping agents were added in this stage for those samples containing natural capping agents. For this, Starch, corn, and rice first grind and their flour were dissolved in 20 mL hot DI water. While above mixture sonicated under ultrasonic, precipitating agent which was Tetraethylenepentamine (TEPA), ethylenediamine (en) or ammonia was added dropwise. After sonication, the product was washed by ethanol and water and dried at 70 C for 24 h. Obtained products were calcinated in different temperatures for 2 h. In this research, we studied the effect of the precipitating agent, calcination temperature, sonication time and power. Experimental details could be found in Table 1 .

Crystal structures of as-prepared LaFeO3 nanostructures were studied by X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). Scanning electron microscope (SEM) was used to study the morphology of LaFeO3 nanostructures. Energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) was employed as more evidence for the successful synthesis of LaFeO3 nanostructures. The optical properties of LaFeO3 nanostructures were investigated by Diffuse Reflectance Spectroscopy (DRS). Also, the electrochemical property of LaFeO3 was studied by cyclic voltammetry (CV). A three electrode system comprising of a glassy carbon electrode (GCE) (Metrohm, Switzerland), a saturated Ag/AgCl (3.0 M KCl) (Metrohm, Switzerland) and Pt wire (Metrohm, Switzerland) were used as working, reference and counter electrode, respectively. The cyclic voltammetry was run by using a probe solution of K3(Fe(CN)6)/K4(Fe(CN)6) in the 0.1 M phosphate buffer solution (pH 7.0) and applying a scan rate of 0.1 V/S.

LaFeO3 was prepared by the sonochemistry method. In the sonochemistry method, the energy that arises from acoustic cavitation could be used as activation energy to drive a chemical reaction. The formation, growth, and implosive collapse of bubbles in a liquid arise temperature to about 5000 K. This energy helps to form our products. LaFeO3 is a common perovskite type oxide with unique properties which make it a promising candidate as visible driven photocatalyst [29,30]. LaFeO3 shows interesting physical and chemical properties. LaFeO3 nanostructures could absorb visible and UV light and generate highly active radicals through AOP mechanism. This is a green reaction with no toxic byproducts. These generated radicals could be used to purify water containing microorganisms and organic contaminants. Here this property was used and removal of organic pollutants.

Calcination temperature was studied as the first parameter which affects the quality of LaFeO3 nanostructures. Fig. 1 illustrates the XRD of samples 1–4. Sample 1–4 was designed to study the effect of calcination temperature on the purity of final products. Fig. 1a shows the XRD pattern for a sample without calcination. XRD pattern shows good agreement with La(OH)3 (JCPDS No. 36-1481). It seems Fe formed as an amorphous oxide phase. It seems the intermediate phase was achieved by calcinating the sample at 500 °C for 2 h (Fig. 1b). Pure LaFeO3 was formed when the calcination temperature increased to 600 °C. As seen in Fig. 1c, as-prepared LaFeO3 shows good agreement with JCPDS No. 88-0641. According to the JCPDS No. 88-0641 LaFeO3 was crystallized in an orthorhombic system with space group: Pnma. The average crystal size was 14 nm according to the Scherer equation. Calcination temperature of 700 °C was studied as well as. Fig. 1d illustrates still pure LaFeO3 was formed at 700 °C. The average crystal size of 19 nm was calculated for this sample. Based on these results, we chose 600 °C as the optimum calcination temperature for further study, because smaller and pure LaFeO3 was formed at this temperature.

The effects of natural and green capping agents on the purity of final products are presented in Fig. 2 a–c. As can be seen in Fig. 2a, pure LaFeO3 was achieved when rice was used as a capping agent. It shows good agreement with reference card 01-075-0541. Fig. 2b shows a mixture of LaFeO3 and Fe2O3 was formed when starch was used as a capping agent. When corn was used as a capping agent (sample 13), pure LaFeO3 nanostructures were obtained (Fig. 2c). We consider sample 13 as the best sample. It seems starch reduces some of the Fe3+ ions to Fe2+. Therefore we observed Fe2O3 as impurity when starch was used as a capping agent.

Sonication time and power are two important parameters that influence the morphology of final products. Fig. 3 a–c shows the effect of sonication time on the morphology of LaFeO3 nanostructures. Rods like LaFeO3 nanostructures with an average length of 100 nm were formed when sonication time was 20 min (Fig. 3a). It seems LaFeO3 nano-rods divided into smaller pieces by increasing sonication power to 30 min (Fig. 3b). As can be seen in Fig. 3c, particles with 20–30 nm in diameters were formed when sonication time was 40 min.

To investigate the effect of on sonication power on the morphology of final products, LaFeO3 nanostructures were fabricated under 40, 60, and 80 W. Related SEM images are illustrated in Figs. 4 a–d and 3b. As can be seen in Fig. 4a, b aggregated rods were formed when sonication power adjust to 40 W. By increasing power to 60 W, LaFeO3 nanorods were formed (Fig. 3b). It seems to change sonication power to 80 W cause broken nanorods and produce particles with 20–80 nm in diameters (Fig. 4c, d).

Fig. 5 a–d show the effect of a precipitating agent (NH3 and en) on the morphology and size of products. When NH3 was used as a precipitating agent, rod like LaFeO3 nanostructures were formed with wide size distributions (Fig. 5a, b). By changing the precipitating agent to TEPA, more uniform rod like LaFeO3 nanostructures were formed (Fig. 3b). Fig. 5c, d shows changing precipitating agent to en leads to form more aggregated rod like LaFeO3 nanostructures.

All the above samples were prepared without capping agents. In the next step, we study the effect of natural capping agent including rice, starch, and corn flour. Using rice as a capping agent leads to produce nanoparticles with 20–60 nm in diameters (Fig. 6 a). When starch was used as a capping agent, it seems particles stuck together to form a small chain (Fig. 6b). Fig. 6c shows that sponge like LaFeO3 nanostructures were obtained when corn was used as a capping agent. We can conclude it is possible to change the morphology and size of LaFeO3 nanostructures by using natural capping agent.

Fig. 7 shows the TEM images LaFeO3 nanostructures prepared by using corn. As can be seen in Fig. 7, LaFeO3 nanostructures were formed with an average of 20 nm in size. Fig. 7a, b show how these particles form rod like structures.

Fig. 8 a–d show the EDX results for samples without capping agents, with corn, starch, and rice as capping agents, respectively. These results indicated samples are containing La, Fe, and O which could be assigned to LaFeO3. La peak in a sample without capping agent shows a higher intensity.

Fig. 9 a displays the FTIR spectra obtained from LaFeO3 powders by using corn as a capping agent. The peak at 557 cm−1 could be assigned to the Fe–O stretching vibration, being characteristics of the octahedral FeO6 groups in the perovskite compounds [31]. The absorption band at about 1474 and 1629 cm−1 are attributing to the splitting of the ν3 asymmetric stretching of metal carbonates [32]. The broad absorption band in the range of 3429 cm−1 is related to O–H stretching. The carbonate detected by FTIR comes from the absorption of CO2 in the air on the surface of LaFeO3 particles. The surfaces of the LaFeO3 nanostructures were chemically active to the absorption of gases such as CO2, lead to the format of carbonate ions [33]. Observing carbonate species on the surface of perovskite-type oxide is a common phenomenon [34–46].

The CV was measured for LaFeO3 nanostructures by using corn as a capping agent at a scan rate of 0.1 V s−1. Fig. 9b shows two obvious peaks at 0.34 V and 0.17 V, which could be related to Fe2+/Fe3+ redox peaks [37].

The specific surface area of LaFeO3 perovskite was analyzed by N2 adsorption/desorption technology (BET). Adsorption-desorption curve, BET, and BJT of LaFeO3 perovskite by using corn as a capping agent are summarized in Fig. 10 a–c. Adsorption-desorption curve of LaFeO3 perovskite shows this isotherm could be classified to type IV as a mesoporous material (Fig. 10b) [38]. The BET specific surface of LaFeO3 nanostructures was 14.7 m2/g (Fig. 10b). BJH results show that pore size for the sample with corn as a capping agent is about 67 nm (Fig. 10c).

The optical properties of LaFeO3 nanostructures were studied by using DRS. Fig. 11 a, b show the DRS of sample calcinated at 700 °C without capping agents and sample prepared by using corn as a green capping agent. Sample without capping agent show a broad absorption peak from 200 nm to 360 nm (Fig. 11a). Using a capping agent (corn) extends the absorption peak to 400 nm (Fig. 11b). Also, absorption intensity increased by using corn as capping agent. Also, bandgap decreased from 2.33 eV for the sample without a capping agent to 2.2 eV for the sample with a capping agent.

As-prepared LaFeO3 nanostructures were used to purify water containing erythrosine and eriochrome black T. Erythrosine is a red dye that is primarily used for food coloring. Fig. 12 shows the photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3 nanostructures under visible light. These results illustrate the effect of capping agents on the photocatalytic activity of LaFeO3 perovskite. The red curve shows the degradation efficiency for LaFeO3 nanostructures without using a capping agent. It degrades 32% of erythrosine in 240 min. 33% of erythrosine was degraded in the same irradiation time when rice was used as a capping agent (green curve). As can be seen from the purple curve, degradation efficiency increased to 45% by using starch as a capping agent. When corn was used as a capping agent in the synthesis of LaFeO3 nanostructures, degradation efficiency increased to 53% (blue curve). Herein visible power was 125 W and contaminant concentration was 10 ppm.

The chemical structure of erythrosine and eriochrome black T and related solutions before and after treatment are illustrated in Fig. 14 . Erythrosine is a red dye used widely in cosmetics and pharmaceuticals. Eriochrome Black T is an indicator of complexometric titrations. It also functions as an acid-base indicator. Fig. 14a shows the water containing erythrosine before irradiation, after aeration, and after 240 min irradiation. Fig. 14b demonstrates the water containing erythrosine before irradiation, after aeration, and after 90 min irradiation. Finally Fig. 14c illustrates such information for Eriochrome Black T.

In summary, we discover the synthesis of LaFeO3 nanostructures by sonochemical methods. Rods like LaFeO3 nanostructures were formed when sonication time was 20 min. smaller rods and nanoparticles were formed when sonication time increased to 30 and 40 min. Aggregated rods were formed when sonication power was 40 W. By increasing power to 60 W, more separated rods were formed. However, more increasing in sonication power broken the rods and produced particles. NH3, TEPA, and en were used as the precipitating agent. Results show that using TEPA leads to produce more uniform rods like LaFeO3 nanostructures. It is possible to control the morphology and size of products by using natural and green capping agent including rice, starch, and corn. Fe2O3 was observed as an impurity when starch was used as a capping agent. It could happen because starch partially reduces Fe3+ ions to Fe2+ ions. As-prepared LaFeO3 nanostructures show promising photocatalytic activity under both visible UV light. As-prepared LaFeO3 nanostructure degrades 53% erythrosine under visible light with 125 W in power, while it degrades 93.5% of erythrosine under UV light with 400 W in power. It also degrades 84% of eriochrome black T under UV light.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are grateful to the council of Iran National Science Foundation; INSF (97017837) and University of Kashan for supporting this work by Grant No (159271/122).
